title: Ovarian Torsion
chief_complaint: I have sudden severe pain in my right lower abdomen that started while I was exercising. The pain comes and goes in waves but it's getting worse. I'm nauseous and vomited twice. I've had an ovarian cyst before but this is different.

physical_exam.Abdomen: RLQ tenderness with voluntary guarding. No rebound.
physical_exam.Pelvic: Right adnexal: Exquisitely tender, enlarged (6cm), mobile mass. Left adnexa: Normal. Cervical motion: Mild tenderness right side. Uterus normal size.
physical_exam.Nausea: Vomiting present (from peritoneal irritation and vagal stimulation).

hidden_data.doppler_us: Right ovary: Enlarged (6cm) with peripheral follicles (string of pearls appearance from edema). Central cystic mass 5cm (likely dermoid). Absent flow on color Doppler (pathognomonic for torsion). Left ovary: Normal flow.
hidden_data.ct_abdomen: Enlarged right ovary with cystic component. No calcifications (dermoid would have). Inflammatory changes.

correct_diagnosis: Right Ovarian Torsion with Ovarian Cyst (Likely Dermoid) - GYNECOLOGIC EMERGENCY
key_learning_points: Ovarian torsion = twisting of ovary on its vascular pedicle (utero-ovarian ligament + infundibulopelvic ligament) → obstruction of venous return → edema → arterial obstruction → ischemia → necrosis. Time-dependent: Ovarian salvage >90% if detorsion <8h, <10% if >48h
Risk factors: Ovarian mass (most common - dermoid, cystadenoma, functional cyst), Ovarian hyperstimulation (OHSS), Pregnancy, Long utero-ovarian ligament (children, adolescents), Prior torsion

Classic presentation: Sudden onset severe unilateral pelvic pain (often during/after exercise), Intermittent (torsion-detorsion), Nausea/vomiting (90% - vagal stimulation), Low-grade fever. Physical exam: Tender adnexal mass. Absent Doppler flow is diagnostic but NOT sensitive (may have intermittent torsion or dual blood supply from ovarian and uterine arteries)

Diagnosis: Doppler ultrasound (absent flow = diagnostic. Flow present does NOT rule out torsion - can be intermittent or dual supply). CT/MRI if US equivocal. Key is HIGH clinical suspicion in young woman with unilateral pain and ovarian mass. SURGERY SHOULD NOT BE DELAYED FOR IMAGING IF HIGH SUSPICION

Treatment: EMERGENCY LAPAROSCOPIC DETORSION. Untwist ovary (usually 2-3 full rotations). Assess viability after detorsion: Color improves (pink → healthy), Bleeding from cut surface. Ovarian cystectomy (remove cyst) to reduce recurrence risk. Oophorectomy ONLY if necrotic and non-viable (rare - ovary has dual blood supply and is remarkably resilient). Oophoropexy (fix ovary to pelvic sidewall) if recurrent torsion or long pedicle

Fertility preservation: Even bluish-black (ischemic) appearing ovaries often recover function after detorsion. ALWAYS attempt ovarian conservation unless clearly necrotic. Ovarian function preserved in >90% after detorsion. Recurrence risk 5-10% (higher if cyst not removed, long pedicle, or children)
